description: The figure displays true stress (σt)-strain (εt) curves (solid lines) superimposed with strain-hardening rate (dσ/dε)-true strain curves (dotted lines) for a Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C austenitic low-density steel processed at different hot-rolling reductions: HR45 (45%), HR68 (68%), and HR82 (82%). The graph reveals a characteristic three-staged strain-hardening behavior across all samples: an initial sharp drop after yielding (Stage I), followed by a gradual increase to peak values (Stage II), and subsequent continuous decrease until fracture (Stage III). Arrows indicate interrupted tensile test strains at εc ≈ 0.1, 0.2, and 0.4. With increasing hot-rolling reduction, strain-hardening rates progressively increase, maintaining a high level of approximately 2000 MPa over a wide plastic strain range, which contributes to the excellent ductility. This three-staged behavior, distinct from conventional high-stacking-fault-energy (SFE ~73 mJ/m²) FCC metals that show monotonically decreasing rates, supports the mechanism for improved strength-ductility combinations in these steels.
